turns.student: Hi, I'm here to help you. What's been going on?
turns.patient: I've had this crampy pain in my lower left abdomen for a few weeks now, and it's getting worse. To be honest, it's really been interfering with my work - I'm a software engineer at a startup, and I need to focus.

turns.student: That sounds really uncomfortable. I can tell this has been weighing on you.
turns.patient: Yeah, it's been stressing me out big time. The pain is constant, even when I'm not working, but at least there I have a deadline to meet and stuff to keep my mind off it. But at home, it's just... (pauses, taking a deep breath) ...it's always there, you know?

turns.student: I want to make sure we don't miss anything important. When these episodes happen, does your body react in any other way?
turns.patient: Yes, when I have one of these episodes, my bowel movements get a lot worse too - it's like 8-10 times a day, and they're loose to watery with this feeling of urgency. It's really inconvenient because I don't know if I'll make it to the bathroom in time.

turns.student: I know it can be scary to think about, but do you notice any sweating or feeling faint during these episodes?
turns.patient: No, I don't get sweaty or feel like I'm going to pass out. But like I said, I do have this urgent feeling with the diarrhea, which makes me feel really anxious and on edge.

turns.student: Thank you for sharing that. I really appreciate your honesty. Does the discomfort stay in one place or does it spread anywhere?
turns.patient: It stays mostly in my lower left abdomen, but sometimes it can kind of radiate up to my back a bit, on the left side too. It's hard to explain, but it feels like the pain is centered there and then it just sort of spreads outwards from that spot.

turns.student: It must be frightening when symptoms like these happen. Any other areas of your body affected?
turns.patient: No, nothing else really. Just the crampy pain in my lower left abdomen and the diarrhea. It's hard to explain, but it feels like they're connected somehow... (pauses, taking a deep breath) ...the pain makes me anxious about the next bowel movement, which then happens more often and is looser. It's this vicious cycle that's really frustrating.

turns.student: I'm concerned about you. Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Even minor changes?
turns.patient: No, I don't think so... I mean, my abdomen feels kind of tender to the touch, but it doesn't look swollen or anything when I look in the mirror. The only thing that's been noticeable is this weight loss - I've lost a bit of weight over the past few weeks because I haven't been eating as much due to the discomfort and anxiety about having diarrhea all the time.

turns.student: Sometimes when people are going through something like this, they also feel nauseous or have stomach upset. Has that happened to you?
turns.patient: To be honest, I haven't really felt nauseous, but my appetite has definitely decreased because of the discomfort and anxiety about having diarrhea all the time. When I do eat, it's often just something light, like takeout or leftovers, and even that can trigger an episode of diarrhea. It's been really hard to keep a regular eating schedule with this going on.

turns.student: I can see this is taking a toll on you. How has your energy been? Your appetite?
turns.patient: To be honest, my energy levels have been really low lately. I've been feeling fatigued even after getting enough sleep, and it's affecting my work performance in a big way. The diarrhea is also making me feel more tired than usual because of the lack of nutrients, I guess. And like you mentioned, my appetite has definitely decreased, which isn't helping with the energy levels either.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers or night sweats? I know it might seem unrelated, but it helps me understand the full picture.
turns.patient: I've had a low-grade fever a few times per week. It's not super high, just around 37.8 to 38.0 degrees Celsius, but it's been a persistent thing that I've noticed over the past few weeks.

turns.student: Your health really matters to me as your care provider. Can you tell me about any other medical conditions you've been diagnosed with?
turns.patient: I had an appendectomy when I was 16, but that was a long time ago and everything went smoothly after that. Other than that, no major health issues that I'm aware of. (pauses for a deep breath) To be honest, it's been over two years since my last doctor's visit, and I haven't had any regular check-ups or bloodwork done recently.

turns.student: I know medications can be a hassle. Are you taking everything as prescribed, or has anything been hard to keep up with?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been self-medicating with loperamide (Imodium) for diarrhea over the past two weeks, but it hasn't really provided much relief. I've taken it as directed, but the frequency and severity of my bowel movements haven't changed significantly.

turns.student: I don't want to pry, but understanding your lifestyle really helps me help you. How do you eat on a typical day?
turns.patient: Honestly, my eating habits have been pretty irregular lately. With long work hours and stress, I've been grabbing takeout or leftovers whenever I can, mostly because it's quick and easy. I don't really have time to cook at home, so that's what I end up doing most of the time. Sometimes I'll eat a meal with my girlfriend if we're both free, but other than that, it's usually just something I can heat up or grab on the go.

turns.student: It sounds like your life keeps you really busy. Do you get a chance to exercise or stay active?
turns.patient: To be honest, exercise has taken a backseat for me lately because of my schedule and energy levels. I'm a software engineer at a startup, so my work hours are pretty long, and when I do have some downtime, I've been using it to catch up on sleep or rest rather than trying to fit in physical activity. I know it's not ideal, but with the stress and discomfort from this issue, exercising has just fallen by the wayside for now.

turns.student: I hate to ask this, but it's really important — has anyone in your family had serious health problems?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease when she was 30. That's about 10 years ago, and it's been a challenge for our whole family ever since. She's undergone a lot of treatment and has learned to manage her symptoms, but it's definitely had an impact on us all.

turns.student: I'm so sorry to hear that. That must have been incredibly difficult. Do you worry about your own health because of it?
turns.patient: Yeah, I do worry about my own health because of it. My sister's experience with Crohn's has made me more aware of potential gastrointestinal issues and how they can affect daily life. It's scary to think that something like this could happen to me too, especially since it feels like it's been going on for so long already.

turns.student: Given everything you've been through, I want to be thorough. Do you drink alcohol? How do you cope with stress?
turns.patient: I drink 2-3 beers most weeknights, but over the past month, it's increased to 4-5 per night because of how stressed out I am from these symptoms and work in general. It's become a way for me to kind of calm down and take my mind off things, but I know that's not a great solution and it's probably making everything worse.

turns.student: I want to make sure we're being thorough. Do you smoke or use any substances?
turns.patient: I don't smoke, and I only drink alcohol like I mentioned earlier - 2-3 beers most weeknights, but it's been up to 4-5 per night recently due to stress. That's about it for substances - I'm not into any recreational drugs or anything like that.

turns.student: Is there anything you've been hesitant to tell me? I promise there's no judgment here.
turns.patient: Honestly, I guess I've been hesitant to talk about how much stress my job and these symptoms have been causing. It feels like a lot of pressure on my plate, and sometimes it's hard for me to admit that I'm struggling with work-life balance and anxiety because I don't want to seem weak or like I'm not capable of handling the demands of my job. But hearing you say there's no judgment here makes me feel a bit more at ease, so thank you for that.

turns.student: Before we wrap up, is there anything else you're worried about that you want to share?
turns.patient: I'm just worried that it might be something serious... like my sister had with Crohn's disease. I don't know if it's related or not, but hearing about her experience makes me a bit scared of what could be going on with me. It feels like it's been going on for so long already, and I just want to get some answers and start feeling better.
